burial.plot_notes: St Jude's Cemetery, Oakville Halton ON — with husband Percy and children.

narrative_blocks.body: Alice Ruth Whitney married Percy Atkinson Bath April 28, 1886 in Montreal and moved with him to Toronto, then to Oakville Ontario where Percy purchased the lakefront home 'Belaire' built 1856 on Lake Ontario. They raised three sons and a daughter at Belaire and attended St James Anglican Church in town. Two of their sons would serve with the Canadian Forces in World War I — including Captain Edward 'Tod' Osler Bath who was captured at the front in 1915 and died of influenza in London 1918 after release.

burial.plot_notes: St Jude's Cemetery, Oakville Halton ON — with husband Percy and children.

narrative_blocks.body: Alice Ruth Whitney married Percy Atkinson Bath April 28, 1886 in Montreal and moved with him to Toronto, then to Oakville Ontario where Percy purchased the lakefront home 'Belaire' built 1856 on Lake Ontario. They raised three sons and a daughter at Belaire and attended St James Anglican Church in town. Two of their sons would serve with the Canadian Forces in World War I — including Captain Edward 'Tod' Osler Bath who was captured at the front in 1915 and died of influenza in London 1918 after release.

burial.plot_notes: St Jude's Cemetery Oakville Halton ON.

parents.father_research_notes: Henry Bath of Longlands (Swansea Wales, d. Oct 13, 1864) — prominent Welsh social and business leader; died of heart attack 6 months after Percy's birth. Bath family ran Henry Bath & Son. Buried in Quaker meeting house family vault Swansea despite later Anglican family identity.
parents.mother_research_notes: Marian Osler Bath (b.1841 d.?, 21 at marriage to 64-year-old Henry of Longlands Nov 14, 1862) — daughter of physician-turned-author Edward Osler; her family the Oslers were staunch Anglican. The Marian Osler→Percy Atkinson lineage gave a connection to the Sir William Osler Canadian medical dynasty.

career.notes: Acquired interest in genealogy and compiled the Bath family tree. Original copy at Hamilton Public Library.

death.cause: heart defect (excluded from WWI service for this; died at 48)

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Whitney carries his maternal grandfather Nathaniel Stevens Whitney's surname into the Bath family generation.

burial.plot_notes: Alton Cemetery Alton Peel ON — spring burial after Mar 7 1980 service Dods and McNair Funeral Home Orangeville.
St Jude's Cemetery Oakville Halton ON — with parents Alice and Percy and brother Tod.

military_service.notes: Served as Flight Lieutenant (flt lieut) with the Royal Flying Corps. Memorial at St Jude's Anglican Church.

bio.title: Captain
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Capt. Edward Osler 'Tod' Bath (1892-1918), WWI POW who died of influenza after release.

death.cause: influenza and pneumonia (after release from POW camp)

burial.plot_notes: St Jude's Cemetery Oakville — Family Plot, L 8, Range 24. Commemorated at Oakville War Memorial, St. Andrew's College, Upper Canada College, St Jude's Anglican Church Memorial, George's Square (Town of Oakville Memorial Roll).

notable.notable_blurb: WWI POW three years, M.I.D. for escape attempts

education.notes: Graduate of Upper Canada College Toronto.
Graduate of St. Andrew's College Aurora ON.

career.notes: Mortgage Broker by occupation at the time of his attestation in 1914-1915 (per CEF service file B0507-S008).

military_service.unit: 15th Battalion (Canadian Infantry, Central Ontario Regiment), Canadian Expeditionary Force — fed by 48th Highlanders of Toronto militia regiment
battles: Second Battle of Ypres (April 28, 1915 — captured at Staden, Belgium)

camps_chronological.camp: Prince of Wales Hospital, London (admitted for gas poisoning effects)

pow.duration_years: 3.5 (April 1915 - March 1918 in German camps; March-November 1918 Holland internment + UK hospital)

awards.citation: The names of the undermentioned Officers, Warrant Officers, Non-Commissioned Officers and Men have been brought to the notice of the Secretary of State for War, in accordance with the terms of Army Order 193 of 1919, for gallant conduct and determination displayed in escaping or attempting to escape from captivity. Dated 5th May, 1919.

military_service.notes: Reported missing April 28, 1915 — captured by enemy at Staden Belgium during Second Battle of Ypres (when German forces first deployed chlorine gas on Western Front). GSW 3rd finger left hand at capture. POW journey through seven German camps over 3.5 years: Reserve Lazarett Hofgeismar (field hospital, Jun 1915) → Munden (Jul 1915) → Wesel (Nov 1916) → Crefeld (May 1917) → Schwarmstedt (Jul 1917) → Holzminden (Nov 1917). Transferred to neutral Holland for internment via Anglo-German prisoner exchange Mar 18, 1918. Arrived Prince of Wales Hospital London Nov 2, 1918 admitted for effects of gas poisoning. Died of influenza pneumonia Nov 23, 1918 — 12 days post-Armistice — age 26. Buried Family Plot L 8 Range 24 St Jude's Cemetery Oakville. Commemorated at Oakville War Memorial. Mentioned in Despatches 'A' List awarded Jan 30, 1920 (London Gazette Issue 31759) for gallant conduct and determination in escape attempts. Service file B0507-S008 available at LAC. CWGC ID 425227.

narrative_blocks.header: Captured Second Ypres 1915, three camps later
narrative_blocks.body: Captain Edward Osler 'Tod' Bath embarked for France April 1, 1915 with the 15th Battalion Canadian Infantry. He was reported missing April 28, 1915 — captured at Staden Belgium during the Second Battle of Ypres, the same campaign in which German forces first deployed chlorine gas on the Western Front. He arrived at his first German camp with a gunshot wound to the third finger of his left hand.

narrative_blocks.header: Mentioned in Despatches for escape attempts
narrative_blocks.body: After Tod Bath's death the British government Mentioned him in Despatches on the 'A' List — published in the London Gazette Issue 31759, January 30, 1920 — for gallant conduct and determination displayed in escaping or attempting to escape from captivity during his three years in German custody. The award placed him among the small group of Canadian officers formally recognized for escape activity in WWI prisoner camps.

narrative_blocks.header: Gas poisoning and flu killed him post-Armistice
narrative_blocks.body: Released to Holland for internment March 18, 1918 and repatriated to England aboard a hospital ship arriving November 2, 1918, he was admitted to Prince of Wales Hospital London for the effects of gas poisoning. He died of influenza and pneumonia at Endsleigh Palace Hospital London on November 23, 1918 — twelve days after the Armistice — at age 26. He was buried in his family plot at St Jude's Cemetery Oakville and is commemorated on the Oakville War Memorial.

quotes.text: for gallant conduct and determination displayed in escaping or attempting to escape from captivity
quotes.source: London Gazette Issue 31759, January 30, 1920 — Mentioned in Despatches 'A' List citation
quotes.notes: The official citation for Tod Bath's posthumous Mentioned in Despatches award.

sources.label: Canadian Great War Project — Edward Osler Bath
Veterans Affairs Canada Virtual War Memorial

documents.name: B0507-S008 CEF Personnel File
documents.notes: Canadian Expeditionary Force personnel service file, digitized by Library and Archives Canada.

sources.label: Canadian Illustrated News of Montreal, 7 July 1883 obituary

parents.father_research_notes: Family was United Empire Loyalist stock per the 1883 Canadian Illustrated News obituary — refugees from the American Revolution who fled north to British Canada. Not built per Jalapeño Rule.

notable.notable_blurb: Montreal merchant, Telegraph Co founder, Horticultural Society president

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Nathaniel Stevens Whitney (1820-1883), Montreal merchant and civic leader.

quotes.text: Another of Montreal's worthies, the esteemed and widely-beloved gentleman, Nathaniel S. Whitney, has at last gone over to the majority at the comparatively early age of 62 years. Respected by all alike, for his loving and excellent qualities, he will long be missed, and his death leaves a blank in a large circle which will not soon be filled up.
quotes.source: Canadian Illustrated News of Montreal, July 7, 1883
quotes.notes: Obituary excerpt for Nathaniel Stevens Whitney.

military_service.notes: Served in both winters of the Rebellions of 1837-1838 (Lower Canada Rebellion), although not of the age required by law (he was only 17 in 1837). Per the Canadian Illustrated News: 'showed himself a worthy descendant [of United Empire Loyalist stock] by serving in both winters of the rebellion, although not of the age required by law.'

burial.plot_notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal Montreal — Mr. Whitney was himself a trustee of the cemetery.

career.notes: Entered wholesale dry goods business after moving to Montreal at an early age from Frelighsburg.
Subsequently entered the leather business, in which he continued until his death.
The first meeting of the Montreal Telegraph Company at its organization was held in his office — placing him at the founding of one of the earliest Canadian telecommunications enterprises.
Largely instrumental in forming the Montreal Horticultural Society, of which he was latterly president.
Life Governor 'not in name only, but in deeds and activity, as the annual reports have testified' per the Canadian Illustrated News obituary.
Trustee of the Mount Royal Cemetery — where he himself would be buried in 1883.

career.role: Lay delegate to Synod / Governor of Diocesan College
career.organization: Church of England (Anglican Church of Canada)
career.notes: Devoted adherent of the Church of England. Lay delegate to the Synod for many years; member of the executive committee; Governor of the Diocesan College.

narrative_blocks.header: Telegraph Company founded in his Montreal office
narrative_blocks.body: Nathaniel Stevens Whitney came to Montreal at an early age from Frelighsburg Quebec and entered the wholesale dry goods business, subsequently the leather trade, which he continued until his death. The first meeting of the Montreal Telegraph Company at its organization was held in his office — placing him at the founding of one of the earliest Canadian telecommunications enterprises. He was instrumental in founding the Montreal Horticultural Society and served as its latter-day president.

narrative_blocks.header: Life governor MGH, Mount Royal Cemetery trustee
narrative_blocks.body: Whitney was Life Governor of the Montreal General Hospital — not in name only but in deeds and activity, as the annual reports testified — and a trustee of Mount Royal Cemetery, where he himself would eventually be buried. A devoted Anglican, he served for many years as lay delegate to the Synod, executive committee member, and Governor of the Diocesan College. Several times invited to stand as Liberal candidate for his native county, he always declined.

Of United Empire Loyalist stock, Whitney served in both winters of the Rebellions of 1837-1838 at Lower Canada — although he was only seventeen in 1837 and not of the age required by law. He caught the cold resulting in his death while visiting The Hills, his country residence at Frelighsburg where he kept a large stock farm. The Canadian Illustrated News obituary of July 7, 1883 called him 'another of Montreal's worthies, the esteemed and widely-beloved gentleman.'

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Hannah Jane Mills Whitney (1826-1905).

notable.notable_blurb: Montreal mayor died of typhus tending Irish immigrants

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of John Easton Mills (1796-1847), Mayor of Montreal.

burial.plot_notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal Montreal — Plot Section G2, Number G204.

career.notes: Elected December 1846 after a deadlocked March 1846 council vote (Mills 10, incumbent James Ferrier 9 — Ferrier voted for himself twice per existing rules; municipal paralysis ensued until December 1846 when Mills was elected decisively). Less than one year in office before his death from typhus November 12, 1847.
Prominent Montreal merchant prior to election as mayor.

narrative_blocks.header: Mayor caught typhus tending Irish famine immigrants
narrative_blocks.body: John Easton Mills was elected Mayor of Montreal in December 1846 after months of deadlock on the city council. Less than a year into his term, the 1847 typhus outbreak struck Montreal among Irish famine immigrants. He personally organized containment measures and volunteered to tend the sick in the Pointe-Saint-Charles immigrant fever sheds — contracted typhus himself — and died November 12, 1847 after less than one year in office.

narrative_blocks.header: Three of his sons died in 1841
narrative_blocks.body: George at six, Edwin at two, and the infant Albert all died in 1841 — likely an epidemic year inside the household. His daughter Ada would die six decades later at the center of Montreal’s Redpath Mansion Mystery.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Montreal mayor who died caring for immigrants
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Lyman married John Easton Mills January 14, 1826 in Montreal. Her husband became Mayor of Montreal and died in 1847 of typhus contracted while personally caring for Irish famine immigrants in the city's fever sheds. She survived him by thirty-four years, eventually dying at Norwood near London England at age eighty-one in 1881.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sons all died in single 1841 year
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Lyman Mills lost three of her sons in 1841: George at age six, Edwin at age two, and Albert in infancy. The pattern suggests a single epidemic or catastrophic event swept through the Mills household that year. She would lose her husband John Easton Mills to typhus six years later, leaving her with three daughters who all married into the Montreal mercantile elite.

burialCemetery.name: St Jude's Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: St Jude's Cemetery, Oakville Halton Regional Municipality Ontario. Burial site of the Bath family — Alice Ruth Whitney Bath (Hooker descendant) and her husband Percy Atkinson Bath, along with their children including WWI POW Capt. Edward Osler 'Tod' Bath.
